introduction.p:
turned Stokes' theorem into a modeling tool. The theorem said that boundary measurements and interior derivatives are two forms of the same information:
\int_{\partial M}\omega=\int_M d\omega.
Conservation laws added time. A density changes inside a region because flux crosses the boundary or because sources act inside.

subsection.title: Concept map
subsection.p: The main chain of ideas is:
subsection.md: \Downarrow\quad\text{use Stokes' theorem}\quad \int_{\partial E}\Phi_{\mathbf J}=\int_E d\Phi_{\mathbf J}
subsection.p: When
and
this becomes the continuity equation:
The main equations of the chapter fit this pattern.
subsection.md: \begin{array}{c|c|c} \text{Model} & \text{Equation} & \text{Idea} \\ \hline \text{continuity} & \rho_t+\nabla\cdot(\rho\mathbf v)=0 & \text{density transported by a flow}\\ \text{heat} & u_t=\alpha\Delta u & \text{heat flows down the gradient}\\ \text{wave} & u_{tt}=c^2\Delta u & \text{acceleration responds to curvature}\\ \text{equilibrium} & \Delta u=0 & \text{no source, no time change}\\ \text{Maxwell} & d\mathcal D=\mathcal Q,\quad d\mathcal B=0,\quad d\mathcal E+\mathcal B_t=0,\quad d\mathcal H-\mathcal D_t=\mathcal J & \text{fields, fluxes, currents, and charge}\\ \text{potential theory} & \Delta u=0 & \text{harmonic balance and least energy} \end{array}

subsection.title: Formula dictionary
subsection.p: For reference, here are the main translations between vector calculus and forms.
subsection.md:
egin{array}{c|c} 	ext{Vector expression} & 	ext{Forms expression} \ \hline 
abla f & df=f_x\,dx+f_y\,dy+f_z\,dz \ 
abla\cdot\mathbf J & d\Phi_{\mathbf J} = (
abla\cdot\mathbf J)\,dx\wedge dy\wedge dz \ \mathbf J\cdot\widehat{\mathbf n}\,dS & \Phi_{\mathbf J} = J_1\,dy\wedge dz+J_2\,dz\wedge dx+J_3\,dx\wedge dy \ \Delta u & d\Phi_{
abla u} = \Delta u\,dx\wedge dy\wedge dz \ 
abla	imes\mathbf E+\mathbf B_t=0 & d\mathcal E+\mathcal B_t=0 \ 
abla\cdot\mathbf B=0 & d\mathcal B=0 \end{array}
subsection.p: The repeated pattern is:

subsection.title: Project 1: conservation of mass in a pipe network
subsection.p:
A small water system has three tanks connected by pipes. Let
M_1(t),\quad M_2(t),\quad M_3(t)
be the mass of water in the tanks.

Water flows from tank 1 to tank 2 at rate
q_{12}=0.4M_1,
from tank 2 to tank 3 at rate
q_{23}=0.3M_2,
and from tank 3 back to tank 1 at rate
q_{31}=0.2M_3.
An external pump adds water to tank 1 at rate 5, and water leaves tank 3 at rate 2.

li.p:
Write the balance law for each tank:
\frac{d}{dt}(\text{mass in tank}) = \text{inflow}-\text{outflow}+\text{source}.

Show that
M_1+M_2+M_3
changes at rate
3.
Explain why the internal pipe flows cancel in the total balance.
Change the external pump rate from 5 to 2. Show that the total mass is then conserved.
Interpret the cancellation of internal flows as a discrete version of the divergence theorem: what leaves one small region enters a neighboring one.

subsection.p:
This project is deliberately finite. It uses tanks instead of a continuum. The same logic becomes
\rho_t+\nabla\cdot\mathbf J=s
when the tanks shrink to tiny regions.

subsection.title: Project 2: heat diffusion on a plate
subsection.p:
A square metal plate occupies
0\le x\le1,\qquad 0\le y\le1.
Its boundary is held at temperature 0. The initial temperature is
u(x,y,0)=\sin(\pi x)\sin(\pi y).

li.p:
Verify that
u(x,y,t)=e^{-2\alpha\pi^2t}\sin(\pi x)\sin(\pi y)
solves
u_t=\alpha(u_{xx}+u_{yy})
and satisfies the boundary condition.

Compute the total heat, up to a constant heat capacity factor:
H(t)=\int_0^1\int_0^1 u(x,y,t)\,dy\,dx.

Now compare with the higher-frequency initial temperature
u(x,y,0)=\sin(3\pi x)\sin(\pi y).
Find the exponential decay rate. Which pattern fades faster?
Give a physical explanation: why should a temperature pattern with sharper spatial oscillation smooth out faster?

subsection.p: This project connects the Laplacian with smoothing. The second derivatives detect sharp bending, and sharp bending drives faster heat flow.

subsection.title: Project 3: incompressible vector fields and stream functions
subsection.p:
In the plane, let
\psi(x,y)
be a differentiable scalar field, and define
\boxed{ \mathbf v=(\psi_y,-\psi_x). }
This construction produces a divergence-free vector field.

li.p:
Verify directly that
\nabla\cdot\mathbf v=0.

Take
\psi(x,y)=\sin x\sin y.
Compute
\mathbf v=(\psi_y,-\psi_x).

Show that the velocity field is tangent to the level curves of \psi. That is, show
\mathbf v\cdot\nabla\psi=0.
Explain why a particle following this flow stays on one level curve of \psi.

Compute the scalar curl of \mathbf v:
\operatorname{curl}_{\mathrm{plane}}\mathbf v = Q_x-P_y,
where
\mathbf v=(P,Q).
Show that
\operatorname{curl}_{\mathrm{plane}}\mathbf v=-\Delta\psi.

subsection.p: The function \psi is called a stream function. Its level curves are stream lines of the incompressible flow. This is another place where a scalar field secretly organizes a vector field.

subsection.title: Project 4: Maxwell's equations in forms and vectors
subsection.p:
This project is a translation exercise. Let
\mathcal E=E_1\,dx+E_2\,dy+E_3\,dz
and
\mathcal B=B_1\,dy\wedge dz+B_2\,dz\wedge dx+B_3\,dx\wedge dy.

li.p:
Compute
d\mathcal E.
Write its three coefficients next to the components of
\nabla\times\mathbf E.

Compute
d\mathcal B.
Show that its coefficient is
\nabla\cdot\mathbf B.

Translate
d\mathcal E+\mathcal B_t=0
into vector notation.

Translate
d\mathcal B=0
into vector notation.

Explain why applying d to
d\mathcal E+\mathcal B_t=0
does not produce a new equation. Use
d^2=0.

subsection.p: The goal is not to memorize two languages. The goal is to see that the vector formulas and the forms formulas describe the same geometry.

subsection.title: Project 5: harmonic functions and boundary data
subsection.p:
Let
R=[0,1]\times[0,1].
A steady temperature u on R satisfies
\Delta u=0.

li.p:
Check that
u_1(x,y)=x
is harmonic.

Check that
u_2(x,y)=y
is harmonic.

Check that
u_3(x,y)=xy
is harmonic.
For each function, describe the boundary temperatures on the four sides of the square.

Choose constants a,b,c and form
u=a u_1+b u_2+c u_3.
Show that u is still harmonic.
Explain why linear combinations of harmonic functions are harmonic.
Use the maximum principle to explain why a harmonic function on R cannot have a strict interior maximum unless it is constant.

subsection.p: This project shows one of the reasons Laplace's equation is manageable: it is linear. Solutions can be added and scaled.

subsection.title: Final reflection: from local change to global balance
subsection.p:
A 1-form measures along curves. A 2-form measures through surfaces. A 3-form measures throughout solids. The exterior derivative moves from one degree to the next:
0\longrightarrow 1\longrightarrow 2\longrightarrow 3.
The generalized Stokes theorem says that an interior derivative is the same information as a boundary measurement:
\int_{\partial M}\omega=\int_M d\omega.
added the last ingredient: time.

Once time enters, Stokes' theorem becomes a bookkeeping machine. What is stored inside a region changes because something crosses the boundary or because something is created inside:
\frac{d}{dt}\int_E \rho\,dV + \int_{\partial E}\Phi_{\mathbf J} = \int_E s\,dV.
Using Stokes' theorem, this becomes the local equation
\rho_t+\nabla\cdot\mathbf J=s.
That one sentence contains a large part of applied mathematics.

Heat chooses
\mathbf J=-k\nabla u.
Waves choose a force law tied to curvature. Equilibrium sets time change to zero. Maxwell's equations choose electric and magnetic forms whose derivatives encode charge, current, flux, and circulation.
The book's central promise was that multivariable calculus is more than a list of formulas. It is a way to move between local and global information:

That is why the same theorem appears under so many names: the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, Green's theorem, Stokes' theorem, the divergence theorem, and finally the generalized Stokes theorem. They are not separate tricks. They are the same idea learning to speak in higher dimensions.
